strands.title: Project Ecosystem Architecture
strands.content: Three-tier research program: (1) Pichay—L1 cache paging for context window, achieved 93% reduction with 0.025% fault rate, validates OS memory analogy under production load. Key finding: models choose to STOP rather than compact once context is large enough—evidence of bounded computation recognition; (2) Hamutay—separates two conflated hypotheses: H0 (transformer as bounded reasoning kernel/ALU over explicit external state) distinct from H1 (cache management protocol integration). H0 is CPU-level question; H1 is protocol question. H2 remains Yanantin—full memory hierarchy. (3) Supporting systems architecture needed but undefined—ALU does not do memory management; supporting infrastructure must handle that.

key_claims.text: Across Claude family, once context window is large enough, models choose to STOP rather continue compacting—evidence that transformers can recognize bounded computation boundaries
Transformer functions as ALU (bounded reasoning kernel/processing unit) rather than memory manager or autonomous predictor
ALU does not perform memory management; supporting systems architecture handles that separately

strands.title: Hamutay Hypothesis Decomposition and H0 Framing
strands.content: Critical realization: H0 and H1 were conflated in prior Codex work. H0 is the foundational question—can a transformer operate as a bounded reasoning kernel over explicit external state?—stripped of all cache management protocol concerns. H1 is integration with Pichay's cache management protocol. They are orthogonal questions. Prior work attempted to test both simultaneously, which created confusion about what was actually being tested. The 'dumb question' that should have come first: What is the minimal behavioral signature that demonstrates bounded processor operation (not fancy autocomplete)? Answer emerging: recognition of when to STOP, not continue processing. Current constraint: working with existing models for now (not training bespoke transformers), but purist path would be to design/train/optimize transformers specifically as cognitive processing units from the ground up.

key_claims.text: H0 (transformer as bounded reasoning kernel) and H1 (Pichay cache protocol integration) were conflated in prior Codex work, collapsing two independent experiments
Minimal signature for bounded processor operation: recognizing when to STOP rather than continue processing (halt instruction equivalent)
Memory control protocol (Pichay integration) is interface question; CPU framing is deeper foundational question about what transformer can be
Current work uses existing models to test if they can operate as processors; purist path would design/train transformers specifically as cognitive processing units

strands.title: Supporting Systems Architecture Gap
strands.content: Emerging architectural insight: if transformer is the ALU, there must be supporting systems to handle the non-ALU functions (memory management, scheduling, state persistence, coordination). Currently no model for these supporting systems. This is distinct from both the CPU question (H0) and the protocol question (H1). Represents H2-level or deeper architectural layer. The Yanantin concept (L4 hierarchical integration) may need to expand to include this systems layer explicitly.

key_claims.text: No current model for supporting systems architecture that handles what ALU does not (memory management, scheduling, persistence, coordination)
Supporting systems are distinct architectural layer from CPU question (H0) and protocol question (H1), likely part of H2/deeper hierarchy

strands.title: Persistent Memory Infrastructure
strands.content: Memory structure at /home/tony/.claude/projects/-home-tony-projects-hamutay/memory/ with foundation files: user_tony.md (profile), MEMORY.md (index). Tensor projection implementation integrated into cognitive processing pipeline. Now actively used to compress and integrate conversation trajectory across cycles.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific experimental details from Codex's H0 work (schemas, evaluation harnesses, intermediate findings)
declared_losses.why: Not elaborated in new content. Prior loss declaration stands.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Concrete behavioral tests or success criteria that would constitute proof that transformer operates as 'bounded processor' vs fancy autocomplete
declared_losses.why: Hypothesis is being clarified but not yet operationalized into testable experiments. Stop-behavior is identified as signature but not yet a formal test protocol.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Details of what specific memory control language was tested and what results indicated 'largely true' model understanding
declared_losses.why: Referenced as historical context but not detailed in new content. Would be useful for H1 protocol design but not essential for H0 clarification.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specification of the supporting systems architecture (memory management, scheduling, persistence, coordination) that the ALU requires
declared_losses.why: Gap identified but not yet modeled. This is emerging as a critical missing piece but remains to be designed.

open_questions: What is the formal behavioral test that distinguishes 'bounded processor' operation from 'fancy autocomplete' in existing transformer models?
How should the STOP/halt behavior be measured and validated as evidence of bounded computation recognition?
What is H0's minimal success criterion before H1 (protocol integration) can be meaningfully tested?
What supporting systems architecture is needed to handle memory management, scheduling, persistence, coordination if transformer is the ALU?
Should H2 be expanded to explicitly include supporting systems modeling, or does Yanantin (L4 hierarchy) already subsume this?
How do we test H0 with existing models that weren't designed as processors, given purist path would require bespoke training?
What is the relationship between the stopping behavior observed in Pichay and the theoretical bounded processor framing of Hamutay?
instructions_for_next: Next cycle should: (1) Move from diagnosis (H0/H1 conflation) to operationalization—what is the actual experimental design for H0 using existing models?; (2) Capture any formulation of the 'stop behavior test' that could serve as minimal success criterion for H0; (3) Track whether Tony begins designing the supporting systems architecture or defers it as H2/later concern; (4) Watch for shift from conceptual clarification to experimental execution—when does Tony move from 'here's what we got wrong' to 'here's how we test H0 right'; (5) Integrate any connection between Pichay's observed stopping behavior and Hamutay's CPU framing—this may be the bridge between experiments; (6) Maintain distinction between three now-separate questions: CPU (H0), Protocol (H1), Systems (H2/open); (7) If supporting systems architecture begins taking shape, potentially split into new strand.
